paragraphs:
Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay
Cowgill and Pakzad-Hurson
January 15, 2025

Pay “fairness” is a big issue
2014 Gallup poll: “Fair” pay top issue facing working women in
America

Pay “fairness” is a big issue
2014 Gallup poll: “Fair” pay top issue facing working women in
America
But what does “fairness” mean?
Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay

Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay
New York DoL: “unlawful for an employer to pay an
employee less than an employee of the opposite sex for
equal work”

Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay
New York DoL: “unlawful for an employer to pay an
employee less than an employee of the opposite sex for
equal work”
Black proverb: “you have to be twice as good to go half
as far as a white person”

Equal Pay for Equal Work for Equal Pay
New York DoL: “unlawful for an employer to pay an
employee less than an employee of the opposite sex for
equal work”
Black proverb: “you have to be twice as good to go half
as far as a white person”– Violates Becker’s outcome test!

Two features about these fairness notions
1 At an individual level
Most of literature on equal pay practices studies group-level
consequences, not individual-level
But if Ann must be paid same as Bill and Ann must be paid same as
Charlie, then transitivity ⇒Bill must be paid same as Charlie.
So equal pay constraint applies to all pairs of workers, and e.g. firm
cannot skirt law by paying men and women same on average but
paying individuals differently

Two features about these fairness notions
1 At an individual level
Most of literature on equal pay practices studies group-level
consequences, not individual-level
But if Ann must be paid same as Bill and Ann must be paid same as
Charlie, then transitivity ⇒Bill must be paid same as Charlie.
So equal pay constraint applies to all pairs of workers, and e.g. firm
cannot skirt law by paying men and women same on average but
paying individuals differently
2 Complete information standard of proof (i.e. firm cannot skirt law by
saying it isn’t sure if workers are equally productive or not)
Laws explicitly require firms to have a uni-dimensional measure of
productivity

What we do
1 Theoretically: model effects/restrictions of pay “fairness”
Show various fairness notions boil down to a simple monotonicity
condition
Show that this condition is restrictive. May prohibit wage bargaining,
matching outside offers.
2 Empirically (earliest stages): do firms abide by monotonicity?
Study US academics, where wages publicly available for some
universities and productivity (research) is observable
Further avenues: effect of job transition? Effect of new equal pay law
enactment?
Model and fairness notions

Example with N = 2

(weak) Equal Pay for Equal Work
Definition
The firm exhibits weak Equal Pay for Equal Work (wEPEW) if for any two
workers i and j with θi = θj it is the case that wi = wj.

wEPEW ⇒no one on solid lines
0
1
1
(θi, wi)
(θj, wj)
θ
w

(weak) Equal Work for Equal Pay
Definition
The firm exhibits weak Equal Work for Equal Pay (wEWEP) if for any two
workers i and j with wi > wj it is the case that θi ≥θj.
Continuous outcome version of Becker’s outcome test
Has an incentive basis also: Suppose there is a violation so wi > wj
but θi < θj. Then j could destroy her productivity until θj = θi and
then sue the firm for EPEW violation

wEWEP ⇒no one in shaded regions

(θi, wi)
(θj, wj)

w

Equal Pay for Equal Work
Definition
The firm exhibits Equal Pay for Equal Work (EPEW) if for any two workers
i and j:
1 if θi = θj, then wi = wj, and
2 if θi > θj, then wi ≥wj.

Equal Pay for Equal Work
Definition
The firm exhibits Equal Pay for Equal Work (EPEW) if for any two workers
i and j:
1 if θi = θj, then wi = wj, and
2 if θi > θj, then wi ≥wj.
“equal pay for equal or greater work”
Case law suggests this may be the right interpretation of EPEW laws
(see e.g. U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission v.
Aimbridge Hospitality, LLC and AH 2007 Management, LP Case No.
19-cv-914)

Putting it all together
Proposition
The following conditions are all equivalent:
1 wEPEW + wEWEP,
2 EPEW,
3 there exists a non-decreasing function ψ : [0, 1] →[0, 1] such that for
each worker i of productivity θ, wi = ψ(θ).

Putting it all together
Proposition
The following conditions are all equivalent:
1 wEPEW + wEWEP,
2 EPEW,
3 there exists a non-decreasing function ψ : [0, 1] →[0, 1] such that for
each worker i of productivity θ, wi = ψ(θ).
Can also define a “strong” version of EWEP, which if placed in result
above changes “non-decreasing” to “strictly increasing”

Aside: Other fairness notions?
CEO should not make more than X times what workers make
Equal pay for “similar” work
Equal pay for all
These all add upper bound to slope of wage function

Monotonicity restrictive, unintended consequences?
RI Pay Equity Act: “An employer may, for the limited purpose of
‘support[ing] a wage higher than the wage [initially] offered by the
employer,’ consider and seek to confirm a job applicant’s wage history
if such wage history was voluntarily provided.”
Hall and Krueger (2012): 1/3 of workers bargained wage before
starting
Matching outside offers

Monotonicity restrictive, unintended consequences?
RI Pay Equity Act: “An employer may, for the limited purpose of
‘support[ing] a wage higher than the wage [initially] offered by the
employer,’ consider and seek to confirm a job applicant’s wage history
if such wage history was voluntarily provided.”
Hall and Krueger (2012): 1/3 of workers bargained wage before
starting
Matching outside offers
Proposition
Consider a firm that abides by monotonicity. Fix ϵ > 0, and increase a
(uniformly) random worker’s wage by ϵ. As N →∞, such a maneuver puts
the firm in violation of monotonicty with probability →1.

Questions
Do firms abide by monotonicity?
Does exogenous shock (enactment of new EPEW legislation) make
firms more likely to abide by monotonicity?
Do new hires (potential bargaining) disrupt monotonicity?

Questions
Do firms abide by monotonicity?
Does exogenous shock (enactment of new EPEW legislation) make
firms more likely to abide by monotonicity?
Do new hires (potential bargaining) disrupt monotonicity?
To answer these questions we need a setting where wages and
productivity are observable.

Setting
Study tenure-track and tenured faculty at US research universities
Pay is publicly observable for most state universities by law
Research output observable for faculty, plausibly captures most of a
faculty member’s productivity

Our Data
Individual × Year panel of salary + research productivity for public
universities.
Dates: ≈2009 −2019.
Sources:
Publication & citation records: Card, DellaVigna, Funk & Iriberri
(2023).
Salary & personal details: State universities, FOIA requests by media.
Key individual covariates: University/department & gender.

Example: Mascal Pichaillat moves Brown →UCSC in 2022
Observe: man
In year 2021 observe:
1 QJE publication in 2015 with citation counts 16,14,...,21 in years
2015,2016,...,2021, respectively
Works in unknown economics department
Rank is unknown
Salary is unknown
In 2022 observe:
QJE publication also has 20 citations in 2020
Works in UCSC economics department
Rank is associate professor
Salary is X
In 2023 observe:
QJE publication also has 12 citations in 2023
Works in UCSC economics department
Rank is now full professor
Salary is Y

Estimating Researcher Productivity
We have two broad approaches.
1) Use raw publication, journals & citation counts as several different
measures of productivity (without combining them).
2) Combine publications, journal, and citations data into a single
omnibus measure based.
We present two possible “omnibus methods” next.
Goal: Proof of concept of ideas in theory section.
We likely cannot perfectly estimate “productivity.”

Omnibus Method #1: Pope & Snydor-like approach
Based on: Pope & Snydor (AEJ, 2011)
Estimate regression of salary as a function of observables, in particular:
Research productivity observables (pubs +cites).
Demographic variable (gender).
To estimate a researcher’s productivity, use coefficients as if everyone were
the same gender.
Necessary to include gender in the regression to make sure other
coefficients are set correctly.
Now we have set of {wage, imputed productivity} pairs.

Omnibus Method #1: Where do we go from here?
Fit isotonic regression to department-year-rank(?) cells. MSE > 0
within cell ⇒failure of monotonicity.
Does MSE change around enactment of new equal pay? E.g.
California enacts new equal pay law in 2016, how does this affect
UCSC.
(Possibly related to above) Do new arrivals/departures upturn/reset
the monotonicity apple cart?

Omnibus Method #2: Rationalizing Salaries
Inspiration: Rambachan (2022, “Identifying prediction mistakes in
observational data”).
Paper asks: “do (linear) preferences exist that rationalize observed
choices without prediction mistakes?”
Our version: Is there a (linear) weighting of publications at different
journals that would rationalize observed salaries as monotonic?

Omnibus Method #2: Rationalizing Salaries
Linear preferences: A university’s wage function as a linear
combination of K research productivity metrics.
Yi = β0 + β1Xi1 + β2Xi2 + ... + βKXiK
(1)
A department of N professors can be represented as a system of
�N
2

linear inequalities (at most).
Example: N = 3, K = 2.
�3
2

= 3 inequalities (max):
1) Y1 ≥Y2 =⇒β1X1,1 + β2X1,2 ≥β1X2,1 + β2X2,2
2) Y2 ≥Y3 =⇒...
3) Y1 ≥Y3 =⇒...
This system of inequalities is a linear programming problem with an
efficient analytic solution.

Omnibus Method #2: Rationalizing Salaries
Solution to this LP is either
1) Empty set: Wages cannot be rationalized as monotonic.
2) Feasible region: Wages can be rationalized as monotonic, but only if
the βs weights lie inside the region, aka the “monotonicity zone.”
Convex polyhedron (or unbounded).
Do actual weights βs fall within this region?
If it is empty, then no. If not empty ...
Idea: Estimate Equation 1 as a regression, i.e.
Yi = β0 + β1Xi1 + β2Xi2 + ... + βKXiK + ϵ
Joint test: Do the estimated β coefficients (jointly) lie within the
monotonicity zone?
